heroOverview: Hypocalcemia is defined as a total serum calcium below 2.1 mmol/L (8.4 mg/dL) or an ionised calcium below 1.1 mmol/L, and it affects roughly 18% of general inpatients and up to 85% of critically ill patients. Calcium drives skeletal muscle contraction, cardiac automaticity, and neurotransmitter release, which explains why even modest falls in ionised calcium produce the hallmark symptom triad of perioral tingling, muscle cramps, and carpopedal spasm. The most common causes worldwide are post-surgical hypoparathyroidism following thyroid or parathyroid surgery, vitamin D deficiency, and hypomagnesemia. Distinguishing these causes early is essential because management differs substantially: supplementing calcium without correcting an underlying magnesium deficiency, for example, is reliably ineffective.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 18% of general hospital inpatients and up to 85% of ICU patients; vitamin D deficiency-related hypocalcemia is the most common form globally
keyStats.demographics: Post-surgical hypoparathyroidism most common in adults aged 40–60 after neck surgery; neonatal hypocalcemia is the most common metabolic disorder of the newborn
keyStats.avgAge: Post-thyroidectomy peak in middle-aged adults; vitamin D-driven deficiency most prevalent in the elderly and populations with limited sun exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Vitamin D deficiency, the leading dietary cause, affects over 1 billion people worldwide, with a substantial subset having ionised calcium at the low-normal or frankly low level

definition: Hypocalcemia (ICD-10: E83.51) is an electrolyte disorder characterised by a total serum calcium below 2.1 mmol/L or an ionised (free) calcium below 1.1 mmol/L. About 45% of circulating calcium is protein-bound, primarily to albumin, so the total calcium must be corrected for albumin when interpreting results: the adjusted calcium rises by 0.2 mmol/L for each 10 g/L fall in albumin below 40 g/L. The ionised fraction is physiologically active and is the preferred measurement in critically ill patients where albumin is often low. Calcium is regulated by parathyroid hormone (PTH), vitamin D (calcitriol), and calcitonin, in a tightly interlocked system; disruption at any point — insufficient PTH secretion, vitamin D deficiency, magnesium depletion, or renal failure — produces hypocalcemia. The condition spans endocrinology, nephrology, and general medicine depending on the aetiology. It is classified as acute (symptomatic, often post-surgical) or chronic (commonly mild, driven by vitamin D deficiency), and the treatment approach differs accordingly. Chronic mild hypocalcemia in vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in elderly, institutionalised, and dark-skinned populations living at higher latitudes.

typesClassification.description: Inadequate PTH secretion — most often from inadvertent damage or removal of the parathyroid glands during thyroid surgery — is the most common cause of acute, severe hypocalcemia in adults.
Insufficient vitamin D impairs intestinal calcium absorption and skeletal calcium mobilisation; this is the most prevalent cause globally and is usually chronic and mild-to-moderate.
Chronic kidney disease reduces calcitriol synthesis and causes hyperphosphatemia that drives calcium into bone; it is a near-universal feature of advanced CKD.

Low magnesium impairs both PTH secretion and end-organ PTH responsiveness, producing a functional hypoparathyroid state; this form is entirely refractory to calcium and vitamin D until magnesium is corrected.

The most common metabolic disorder of newborns; early-onset (within 72 hours) is linked to prematurity, maternal diabetes, and birth asphyxia; late-onset (day 5–7) is associated with high-phosphate cow's milk formula.

primarySymptoms: Perioral numbness and tingling (paraesthesias around the mouth and lips) — classically the first symptom, reflecting heightened excitability of sensory nerve fibres as ionised calcium falls.
Tingling and numbness in the hands and feet, spreading from the fingertips and toes inward as hypocalcemia worsens.
Muscle cramps and aching in the calves, feet, and hands, ranging from mild discomfort to intense involuntary spasm.
Carpopedal spasm — involuntary flexion and adduction at the wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints, producing the characteristic main d'accoucheur (obstetric hand) posture.
Positive Trousseau's sign: carpal spasm induced by inflating a blood-pressure cuff above systolic pressure for three minutes, which is 94% sensitive for hypocalcemia.
Positive Chvostek's sign: ipsilateral facial muscle twitching elicited by tapping the facial nerve just anterior to the ear, seen in approximately 50–75% of hypocalcemia cases.
Generalised muscle weakness and fatigue from impaired excitation-contraction coupling in skeletal muscle.
Laryngospasm — a severe involuntary spasm of the laryngeal muscles producing a high-pitched inspiratory stridor; it is an emergency complication of acute severe hypocalcemia.
Prolonged QT interval on ECG, reflecting impaired cardiac membrane repolarisation; in severe cases, ventricular arrhythmia can occur.
Anxiety, cognitive slowing, and in chronic cases, depression or frank confusion — calcium is required for neurotransmitter release and neural membrane stability.
Dry skin, brittle nails, coarse hair, and, with chronic hypoparathyroidism, cataracts and basal ganglia calcification.
earlyWarningSigns: A tingling or 'electric' sensation around the mouth when taking deep breaths or during emotional stress.
Fingers that cramp or feel stiff when gripping objects, particularly after exercise.
Increased startle reflex or a general sense of nervous tension that seems out of proportion to circumstances.
Unexplained fatigue with an abnormal Trousseau's sign on routine examination.
emergencySigns: Laryngospasm with audible stridor and respiratory distress — an immediate airway emergency requiring IV calcium.
Generalised tonic-clonic seizure in the context of known or suspected hypocalcemia.
Sustained carpopedal spasm affecting the respiratory muscles, causing dyspnoea and cyanosis.
Ventricular arrhythmia or cardiac arrest detected on monitoring, particularly after recent thyroid or parathyroid surgery.
Altered consciousness, confusion, or coma in acute severe hypocalcemia (ionised calcium below 0.7–0.8 mmol/L).

causes.description: The most common cause of acute severe hypocalcemia in adults; inadvertent damage, devascularisation, or removal of the parathyroid glands during total thyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy, or radical neck dissection interrupts PTH secretion, and calcium can fall precipitously within 24–48 hours.

Insufficient dietary intake or sun exposure reduces intestinal calcium absorption, which normally accounts for 30–40% of dietary calcium; serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels below 25 nmol/L are associated with secondary hyperparathyroidism and overt hypocalcemia, particularly in the elderly and in people with malabsorption.

Low magnesium suppresses PTH secretion from the chief cells of the parathyroid gland and simultaneously impairs the renal and skeletal response to whatever PTH is produced; the resulting hypocalcemia is completely refractory until magnesium is replaced.

Advanced CKD reduces calcitriol (active vitamin D) synthesis in the kidney, causes hyperphosphatemia that complexes with calcium, and produces skeletal resistance to PTH; hypocalcemia is present in the majority of patients with eGFR below 30 mL/min.

Released pancreatic lipases saponify peripancreatic fat, forming calcium soaps that chelate ionised calcium; the drop can be severe enough to cause tetany in acute severe pancreatitis and is a marker of disease severity.

Autoantibodies against the calcium-sensing receptor or parathyroid tissue destroy gland function; it is the most common cause of chronic hypoparathyroidism not related to surgery, often occurring as part of autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1 (APS-1).

These potent anti-resorptive agents for osteoporosis sharply reduce the rate at which calcium is mobilised from bone; hypocalcemia develops particularly when vitamin D stores are low at the time of treatment initiation, which is why vitamin D repletion is mandated before starting them.

riskFactors.description: Transient hypocalcemia occurs in 10–50% of patients after total thyroidectomy; permanent hypoparathyroidism affects 1–3%; risk is higher with more extensive neck dissection.
Dark skin pigmentation, limited sun exposure, housebound elderly status, and a diet low in oily fish and fortified foods all reduce vitamin D synthesis and intake; correction eliminates this risk.
The risk of hypocalcemia rises as eGFR falls; calcitriol supplementation is a standard part of CKD management from stage 3b–4 onward.
Low magnesium is among the most correctable causes of hypocalcemia; identifying and treating the magnesium deficit resolves the calcium disturbance in most patients.
Coeliac disease, Crohn's disease, short-bowel syndrome, and bariatric surgery impair calcium and vitamin D absorption; supplementation doses typically need to be higher than standard.
Bisphosphonates and denosumab increase hypocalcemia risk when started without ensuring adequate vitamin D and calcium stores; pre-treatment optimisation is required by prescribing guidelines.
Multiple simultaneous factors — hypomagnesemia, sepsis, multiple blood transfusions, alkalosis, and citrate in blood products — produce hypocalcemia in up to 85% of ICU patients.
APS-1, caused by AIRE gene mutations, features autoimmune destruction of the parathyroid glands alongside adrenal insufficiency and candidiasis, causing permanent hypoparathyroidism from childhood.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40–60 who have undergone total thyroidectomy or parathyroidectomy
Elderly and housebound individuals with vitamin D deficiency and low dietary calcium
Patients with advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD stages 4–5)
People with coeliac disease, Crohn's disease, or prior bariatric surgery
Critically ill patients in intensive care
Newborns, particularly premature infants or those of diabetic mothers
Patients starting bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy without vitamin D optimisation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a serum total calcium, which is adjusted for albumin when protein levels are low using the formula: corrected calcium (mmol/L) = total Ca + 0.02 × (40 – albumin g/L). Ionised calcium measurement from an arterial or venous blood-gas analyser is the most accurate test and is preferred in the ICU or immediately after surgery. Once hypocalcemia is confirmed, the next step is measuring intact PTH: a high PTH points toward vitamin D deficiency, malabsorption, or CKD (secondary hyperparathyroidism), whereas a low or inappropriately normal PTH confirms hypoparathyroidism. Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, phosphate, magnesium, creatinine, and albumin form the standard initial panel and together identify the cause in the majority of cases. An ECG should be obtained in symptomatic patients to check the QTc interval. For autoimmune hypoparathyroidism, anti-calcium-sensing receptor antibodies and the broader APS-1 panel may be requested. In post-surgical patients, hourly or twice-daily calcium monitoring in the first 48 hours catches the rapid drop that precedes symptoms. The key clinical signs — Chvostek's and Trousseau's — should be elicited at the bedside whenever hypocalcemia is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening test; correcting for albumin prevents misclassification in patients with low protein levels.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results available within hours as part of a routine metabolic panel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most accurate measure of physiologically active calcium; preferred in the ICU, post-operative patients, and when acid-base disturbance may affect protein binding.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample taken from an artery or vein and analysed immediately on a bedside or laboratory blood-gas machine.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes hypoparathyroidism (low PTH) from secondary hyperparathyroidism (high PTH) due to vitamin D deficiency or CKD, which directs treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn from the same blood sample as calcium; PTH is labile and should be transported on ice to preserve accuracy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies vitamin D deficiency (below 50 nmol/L) as the cause of secondary hyperparathyroidism and hypocalcemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results typically available within 1–2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High phosphate alongside low calcium and low PTH confirms hypoparathyroidism; low magnesium identifies the correctable upstream cause of functional hypoparathyroidism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same routine blood panel as calcium; no additional samples required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the QTc interval, which is prolonged in hypocalcemia and predicts cardiac arrhythmia risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-electrode, 10-second painless recording interpreted by the treating clinician.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Guides long-term calcium supplementation dosing in chronic hypoparathyroidism; excessive urine calcium raises the risk of kidney stones and nephrocalcinosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: All urine collected over 24 hours into a container provided by the laboratory.

treatmentOverview: The urgency and route of treatment are determined by symptom severity and the underlying cause. Acute, symptomatic hypocalcemia — presenting with tetany, laryngospasm, seizures, or QT prolongation — is treated as an emergency with intravenous calcium gluconate: 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate (providing 93 mg elemental calcium) over 10–20 minutes, followed immediately by a continuous calcium gluconate infusion (typically 10–20 g diluted in 1 litre of saline or glucose over 24 hours) until oral therapy is tolerated and serum calcium is stable. Calcium chloride, which delivers three times more elemental calcium per unit volume, is preferred during cardiac arrest but is caustic to veins and reserved for central or emergent peripheral access. For mild-to-moderate chronic hypocalcemia, oral calcium carbonate or calcium citrate at 1,000–2,000 mg elemental calcium per day in divided doses is first-line, combined with calcitriol (0.25–1 mcg daily) in hypoparathyroidism or CKD where renal vitamin D activation is impaired. In simple vitamin D deficiency with intact PTH response, standard vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) at 1,000–4,000 IU daily normalises calcium in most patients over 4–12 weeks. Hypomagnesemia-driven hypocalcemia requires magnesium replacement before calcium levels will respond; adding calcium without fixing magnesium produces no sustained benefit. Recombinant PTH (palovarotide/PTH 1–34 or PTH 1–84) is now available for chronic hypoparathyroidism uncontrolled on conventional therapy, reducing the calcium and calcitriol doses needed and the risk of hypercalciuria. All patients on calcium and calcitriol require regular monitoring of serum calcium (every 3–6 months) and urinary calcium to prevent hypercalcemia or nephrocalcinosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous calcium gluconate
medicalTreatments.description: 10% calcium gluconate 10 mL over 10–20 minutes is the emergency first-line treatment for symptomatic hypocalcemia with tetany, seizures, laryngospasm, or QT prolongation; followed by a continuous infusion of 0.5–1.5 mg/kg/hour elemental calcium titrated to serum levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls acute tetany and laryngospasm in approximately 90% of patients within minutes of IV bolus.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral calcium carbonate
medicalTreatments.description: 1,000–2,000 mg elemental calcium daily in 2–3 divided doses with meals; the most cost-effective chronic maintenance treatment. Calcium carbonate requires gastric acid for absorption and is taken with food.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains serum calcium in the low-normal range in approximately 70–80% of patients with chronic hypoparathyroidism when combined with calcitriol.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcitriol (active vitamin D)
medicalTreatments.description: 0.25–1 mcg orally once or twice daily; bypasses the renal 1-alpha-hydroxylation step that is absent in hypoparathyroidism and CKD, making it far more effective than standard vitamin D in those conditions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises intestinal calcium absorption and raises serum calcium by approximately 0.2–0.5 mmol/L in most patients with hypoparathyroidism when combined with oral calcium.

medicalTreatments.name: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3)
medicalTreatments.description: 1,000–4,000 IU daily for vitamin D deficiency-related secondary hyperparathyroidism; higher loading doses (50,000 IU weekly) are used in severe deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises 25-hydroxyvitamin D into the sufficient range and normalises calcium in approximately 80–90% of patients with simple vitamin D deficiency over 8–12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Recombinant PTH 1–84 (natpara)
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous injection of recombinant PTH provides the missing hormonal drive in chronic hypoparathyroidism, allowing lower doses of calcitriol and calcium supplements; approved for adults with hypoparathyroidism inadequately controlled on standard therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves target calcium control with at least 50% reduction in calcitriol dose in approximately 53% of treated patients in pivotal phase 3 trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Magnesium replacement
medicalTreatments.description: When hypomagnesemia is driving the hypocalcemia, IV or oral magnesium replacement is the definitive treatment; calcium and vitamin D are given concurrently but calcium will not normalise durably until magnesium stores are replenished.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores calcium to normal in essentially 100% of hypomagnesemia-induced hypocalcemia cases once magnesium is corrected.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary calcium optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing intake of dairy products, fortified plant milks, canned fish with bones, white beans, and tofu provides 200–300 mg elemental calcium per serving; dietary calcium is preferred over supplements because it carries less cardiovascular risk at equivalent doses.

alternativeTreatments.name: Safe sun exposure for vitamin D
alternativeTreatments.description: 10–30 minutes of midday sun on exposed forearms and legs several times per week generates roughly 1,000 IU of vitamin D3 in lighter-skinned adults; this is an important adjunct to supplements for vitamin D-driven hypocalcemia in populations with limited outdoor activity.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be involved in any case of post-surgical or autoimmune hypoparathyroidism, since long-term calcitriol and calcium dosing requires careful titration to avoid hypercalciuria and nephrocalcinosis. A nephrologist is essential when hypocalcemia is driven by CKD, where renal bone disease management is complex. Specialist review is also warranted for recurrent or refractory hypocalcemia without a clear cause, for consideration of PTH replacement therapy, and in paediatric cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist with a specific interest in calcium and parathyroid disorders — ideally one who manages post-surgical hypoparathyroidism regularly and has experience with calcitriol dosing and urinary calcium monitoring. For CKD-related disease, a nephrologist skilled in renal bone disease is preferable. Ask whether the practice has access to ionised calcium and 24-hour urinary calcium measurement, which are essential for safe long-term management.
preventionStrategies: Ensure vitamin D status (25-OH-D) is above 50 nmol/L before starting bisphosphonates or denosumab, and maintain it throughout treatment.

Check calcium and PTH levels 24–48 hours after total thyroidectomy or parathyroid surgery; prophylactic calcium and calcitriol for 2–4 weeks post-operatively substantially reduces the rate of symptomatic hypocalcemia.
Treat hypomagnesemia promptly whenever it is found, since correcting magnesium prevents most cases of secondary hypocalcemia.
Elderly, housebound, and dark-skinned individuals living above 35° latitude should take at least 800–1,000 IU vitamin D3 daily year-round.
Screen for hypocalcemia at the initiation of CKD stage 3b management and monitor every 6 months as kidney function declines.
Use calcium citrate rather than carbonate in patients with achlorhydria or on PPIs, since citrate does not require acid for absorption.
lifestyleModifications: Eat 2–3 servings of calcium-rich food daily: dairy, fortified plant milks, tinned salmon or sardines with bones, tofu, and white beans.
Get safe sun exposure (10–30 minutes on forearms and face) several times per week when possible to support endogenous vitamin D production.
Limit caffeine and alcohol, which increase urinary calcium losses.
Avoid very high-fibre diets (particularly wheat bran) taken with calcium supplements, since phytate reduces calcium absorption.
If you have had thyroid or parathyroid surgery, attend all post-operative blood test appointments and report perioral tingling or hand cramps immediately.
Space calcium supplements and iron or thyroid hormone by at least two hours to prevent mutual absorption interference.

recommended: Dairy foods — milk, yoghurt, and hard cheese — which each provide 200–300 mg elemental calcium per serving
Calcium-set tofu and edamame as plant-based sources
Canned fish with soft bones (sardines, salmon) — a serving delivers roughly 250 mg calcium
Calcium- and vitamin D-fortified plant milks and breakfast cereals
Almonds and bok choy for non-dairy calcium without significant oxalate binding
Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, tuna) and egg yolk for dietary vitamin D
avoid: Very large amounts of spinach, rhubarb, or Swiss chard eaten simultaneously with calcium-containing foods — their oxalate binds calcium in the gut, reducing absorption
Excess caffeine (more than 4 cups of coffee daily), which increases renal calcium excretion
High-sodium foods, which increase obligatory urinary calcium losses
Fizzy cola drinks, which contain phosphoric acid and may worsen calcium balance with chronic high intake

exerciseGuidelines: Weight-bearing exercise — walking, jogging, resistance training — is encouraged in patients with chronic hypocalcemia because it stimulates bone formation and improves calcium retention in the skeleton. During acute symptomatic hypocalcemia, strenuous exercise should be avoided as hyperventilation (which worsens alkalosis and drives ionised calcium lower) can precipitate tetany. Once calcium is corrected and stable, normal activity including sport is safe. Outdoor exercise also promotes sun exposure, supporting endogenous vitamin D synthesis.
dailyManagement: Take calcium supplements in divided doses (500 mg or less elemental calcium at a time) to optimise absorption.
Take calcitriol consistently at the same time each day; doses should not be doubled if a dose is missed.
Keep a symptom diary noting tingling, cramps, or muscle tightness so trends can be identified before levels fall dangerously.
Attend blood test appointments every 3–6 months for calcium, phosphate, and urine calcium when on long-term calcitriol.
Report palpitations, throat tightness, or difficulty breathing immediately — these can reflect cardiac or airway complications of falling calcium.
Carry a wallet card or wear a medical alert bracelet identifying hypoparathyroidism so emergency teams can act quickly.
prognosis: The prognosis of hypocalcemia depends entirely on the underlying cause. Acute post-surgical hypocalcemia resolves in the majority of cases within days to weeks as parathyroid function recovers; permanent hypoparathyroidism, affecting about 1–3% of total thyroidectomies, requires lifelong treatment but is compatible with a normal quality of life with careful monitoring. Vitamin D-deficiency hypocalcemia resolves completely with supplementation in most patients over 8–12 weeks. Hypomagnesemia-driven hypocalcemia corrects as soon as magnesium is repleted. In CKD, hypocalcemia is managed rather than cured; outcomes are primarily determined by the trajectory of kidney disease. Inadequately treated chronic hypoparathyroidism carries risks of cataracts, basal ganglia calcification, nephrocalcinosis, and reduced quality of life, which is why titrated long-term therapy and periodic imaging are recommended by the European Society of Endocrinology.
recoveryTimeline: Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia responds to IV calcium gluconate within minutes; the infusion is typically continued for 12–24 hours. Post-surgical transient hypoparathyroidism usually resolves over 2–6 weeks as parathyroid function returns, and supplements are weaned accordingly. Vitamin D deficiency-related hypocalcemia normalises over 8–12 weeks of supplementation. In permanent hypoparathyroidism, calcium and calcitriol are continued indefinitely, but most patients achieve stable levels within 4–8 weeks of dose titration.
complications: Laryngospasm — severe involuntary contraction of the laryngeal muscles causing stridor and respiratory failure; a medical emergency requiring immediate IV calcium.
Seizures — generalised or focal seizures from cortical irritability; may be misdiagnosed as idiopathic epilepsy when hypocalcemia is chronic and mild.
Ventricular arrhythmia — QT prolongation from low ionised calcium can degenerate into polymorphic ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation.
Cataracts — posterior subcapsular cataracts develop in 50% of patients with longstanding hypoparathyroidism and can impair vision significantly.
Nephrocalcinosis and kidney stones — excess calcium and calcitriol therapy, or untreated hypercalciuria, deposits calcium in renal tubules and may cause permanent kidney injury.

Basal ganglia calcification (Fahr syndrome-like changes) — symmetrical calcium deposits in the basal ganglia occur in chronic hypoparathyroidism and can cause parkinsonism or neuropsychiatric symptoms.

supportResources.name: Hypoparathyroidism Association
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organisation providing education, support groups, and research updates for people with hypoparathyroidism and chronic hypocalcemia.

supportResources.name: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
supportResources.description: Detailed review of calcium physiology, dietary sources, recommended intakes, and evidence for supplementation from the U.S. National Institutes of Health.

supportResources.name: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Consumers
supportResources.description: Plain-English guide to vitamin D sources, recommended intake, and health effects relevant to people managing calcium disorders.

sources.title: Bilezikian JP et al. Guidelines for the management of hypoparathyroidism. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2022
Shoback D et al. Presentation of hypoparathyroidism: Etiologies and clinical features. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2016
Cooper MS, Gittoes NJ. Diagnosis and management of hypocalcaemia. BMJ, 2008
NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Bollerslev J et al. European Society of Endocrinology Clinical Guideline: Treatment of chronic hypoparathyroidism in adults. European Journal of Endocrinology, 2015
Nair R, Maseeh A. Vitamin D: The 'sunshine' vitamin. Journal of Pharmacology & Pharmacotherapeutics, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypomagnesemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause tetany and positive Chvostek/Trousseau signs, but hypomagnesemia is an upstream cause of hypocalcemia rather than a separate diagnosis; they co-exist so frequently that magnesium must always be checked when hypocalcemia is found.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypercalcemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypercalcemia causes the opposite neurological picture — muscle weakness, confusion, and nausea — rather than neuromuscular excitability; it can be confused during initial assessment when a calcium level is not yet available.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal tubular acidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RTA causes a metabolic acidosis that may be accompanied by calcium and magnesium wasting; the acid-base profile and urine pH distinguish it from pure hypocalcemia.

relatedConditions.name: Hypomagnesemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypomagnesemia is one of the most common and entirely correctable causes of hypocalcemia; always check magnesium when calcium is low.

relatedConditions.name: Hypercalcemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia are opposite poles of calcium dysregulation, often sharing the same underlying conditions (e.g., parathyroid disorders) as root causes.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic hypocalcemia and secondary hyperparathyroidism drive bone resorption, contributing to reduced bone mineral density and fracture risk.

relatedConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcemia; surgical cure can produce post-operative hypocalcemia (hungry bone syndrome) as bone rapidly re-mineralises.

faqs.question: What is hypocalcemia?
faqs.answer: Hypocalcemia means your blood calcium level is below normal. Calcium controls muscle contraction, heart rhythm, and nerve signalling, so low levels cause tingling, muscle cramps, and in severe cases, seizures or dangerous heart rhythms.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of low calcium?
faqs.answer: The most common symptoms are perioral tingling (numbness around the mouth), hand and foot cramps, carpopedal spasm (hands clench involuntarily), and muscle weakness. Severe hypocalcemia causes laryngospasm, seizures, or abnormal heart rhythms.

faqs.question: What causes hypocalcemia?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are post-surgical hypoparathyroidism (after thyroid surgery), vitamin D deficiency, hypomagnesemia, and chronic kidney disease. Each impairs the body's ability to maintain normal calcium levels through different mechanisms.

faqs.question: How is hypocalcemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A blood test measuring serum calcium, corrected for albumin, is the first step. The cause is identified by measuring intact PTH, vitamin D, magnesium, phosphate, and kidney function. An ECG checks for QT prolongation, a cardiac risk of low calcium.

faqs.question: How is hypocalcemia treated?
faqs.answer: Acute severe hypocalcemia is treated with IV calcium gluconate in hospital. Mild-to-moderate chronic hypocalcemia is managed with oral calcium and vitamin D supplements. The underlying cause — vitamin D deficiency, low magnesium, or kidney disease — must also be treated.

faqs.question: What is a Trousseau's sign?
faqs.answer: Trousseau's sign is a clinical test for hypocalcemia. A blood-pressure cuff is inflated above systolic pressure for three minutes; if the hand contracts into a claw-like posture (carpopedal spasm), the test is positive. It is 94% sensitive for hypocalcemia.

faqs.question: Can hypocalcemia cause seizures?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severely low calcium increases the excitability of nerve cells and can trigger generalised or focal seizures. This is most common in acute post-surgical hypoparathyroidism or severe vitamin D deficiency. IV calcium gluconate is the emergency treatment.

faqs.question: Why does thyroid surgery cause low calcium?
faqs.answer: The four parathyroid glands sit directly behind the thyroid gland. During a total thyroidectomy, they can be accidentally damaged, devascularised, or removed. Without these glands, PTH falls and calcium drops rapidly — sometimes within 24 hours of surgery.

faqs.question: Is low calcium the same as low vitamin D?
faqs.answer: No, but they are closely linked. Vitamin D deficiency reduces intestinal calcium absorption, which can eventually lower calcium levels. However, hypocalcemia has multiple other causes. A vitamin D level and PTH test are needed to identify the root cause.

faqs.question: Can low magnesium cause low calcium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Magnesium is required for the parathyroid glands to secrete PTH and for the body to respond to PTH. When magnesium is low, calcium falls and cannot be corrected by calcium or vitamin D supplements alone — magnesium must be replaced first.

faqs.question: How much calcium should I take daily?
faqs.answer: Adults need 1,000 mg of elemental calcium daily; this rises to 1,200 mg for women over 50 and men over 70 (NIH). Food is the preferred source. If supplements are needed, doses of 500 mg or less at a time are better absorbed than single large doses.

faqs.question: Which form of calcium supplement is best?
faqs.answer: Calcium carbonate is the most cost-effective and widely used form — take it with food because it needs stomach acid to dissolve. Calcium citrate is absorbed without food and is better for people on PPIs or with low stomach acid. Both provide similar benefit when taken correctly.

faqs.question: Can hypocalcemia affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. Low calcium prolongs the QT interval on an ECG, which can lead to a dangerous irregular heartbeat called ventricular arrhythmia. People with hypocalcemia and cardiac symptoms — palpitations, dizziness — need urgent evaluation and cardiac monitoring.

faqs.question: What is calcitriol and when is it needed?
faqs.answer: Calcitriol is the active form of vitamin D made by the kidneys. It is prescribed instead of standard vitamin D when the kidneys cannot activate regular vitamin D supplements — particularly in hypoparathyroidism and chronic kidney disease.

faqs.question: Is hypocalcemia dangerous during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Low calcium in pregnancy can impair foetal bone development and increase the mother's risk of tetany and arrhythmia. Vitamin D and calcium requirements increase during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Any suspected deficiency should be evaluated and treated promptly.

faqs.question: How do I know if my calcium is too low?
faqs.answer: Perioral tingling, finger or toe numbness, involuntary hand cramping, and muscle weakness are early warning signs. A simple blood test confirms the diagnosis. If you experience throat tightness, difficulty breathing, or a seizure, seek emergency care immediately.

faqs.question: What foods are high in calcium?
faqs.answer: Dairy products (milk, yoghurt, cheese), calcium-set tofu, canned sardines and salmon with bones, calcium-fortified plant milks, white beans, almonds, and bok choy are among the best dietary sources of calcium.

faqs.question: Can I take too much calcium?
faqs.answer: Yes. The tolerable upper intake level for adults is 2,500 mg/day from all sources. Too much calcium increases the risk of kidney stones, constipation, and — from supplements specifically — may raise cardiovascular risk. Aim to meet needs through diet first, using supplements only to fill gaps.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for low calcium?
faqs.answer: See an endocrinologist if you have had thyroid or parathyroid surgery, if your calcium remains low despite standard supplements, or if you have hypoparathyroidism requiring calcitriol. A nephrologist manages hypocalcemia related to kidney disease.

faqs.question: Can hypocalcemia be permanent?
faqs.answer: Permanent hypoparathyroidism after surgery affects about 1–3% of total thyroidectomy patients and requires lifelong calcium and calcitriol. However, vitamin D deficiency-related hypocalcemia is completely reversible with supplementation, and most other causes are treatable.

metaDescription: Hypocalcemia (low calcium) causes tingling, muscle cramps, and tetany. Learn the causes, diagnosis, and calcium and vitamin D treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
